title: Share of one-year-olds vaccinated against measles
subtitle: Share of one-year-olds who have received the first dose of the measles vaccine (MCV1).
caption:
Share of one-year-olds vaccinated against measles

Share of one-year-olds who have received the first dose of the measles vaccine (MCV1).

The choropleth map displays the percentage of one-year-olds who received the first dose of the measles vaccine across countries in 2024, using a continuous color scale ranging from pale yellow for 0% coverage to dark blue for 100% coverage. The highest vaccination rates, falling in the 95% to 100% band, appear across most of North America, including the United States and Canada, as well as throughout Europe, with countries like France, Germany, and the United Kingdom showing full coverage. Russia, China, and Australia also sit in this top tier. In South America, Brazil and Argentina display high coverage in the 90-95% range, while neighboring countries show slightly lower rates. The middle of the distribution, represented by teal and medium blue shades indicating 70% to 90% coverage, includes nations such as India, Mexico, and Indonesia. The lowest vaccination shares, visible in pale yellow and light green bands representing 0% to 60%, concentrate heavily in central and western Africa, including Chad, the Central African Republic, and Somalia, as well as parts of the Middle East like Yemen. Greenland and some smaller island nations appear in gray, indicating no data is available. Measles is a highly contagious viral disease, most common in young children, with effects including blindness, inflammation of the brain, severe diarrhea, and severe respiratory infections such as pneumonia. The geographic distribution shows a clear divide where high-income regions and large emerging economies maintain near-universal coverage, while specific regions in Africa and conflict-affected areas remain below the threshold required for herd immunity.
